titleEn: White Hair Woven as a Veil

verses.sadr: ما اسود حتى ابيض كالكرم الذي
verses.ajuz: لم يأن حتى جيء كيما يقطفا

verses.sadr: ما كان يخطر قبل ذا في فكره
verses.ajuz: في البدر قبل تمامه أن يكسفا

verses.sadr: كم من شماتة حاسد إن أنت لم
إن أنت لم تفضل ولم تر أنني
verses.ajuz: أهل له فأنا أرى أن تنصفا

verses.sadr: ما عذر من كان النوال مطيعه
الله جارك أن تحول وأن يهي
لا تصرفن نداك عمن لم يدع
ثقف فتي في الجود تلق قصائدا

verses.ajuz: لم يفن ما أبقى الثناء المضعفا

translationEn: White hair has woven for him a thick, lowered veil of pure white, covering both his temples and half of him. Time looked upon him and cut short what lay before him, gazing like a distressed beloved in grief and longing. He did not whiten until he had blackened, like a vine that is not ready until it is brought to be harvested. When adversities stripped away their darkness with their whiteness, they toyed with him and bleached him entirely. It never crossed his mind before this — just as the full moon before its completion never thinks it will be eclipsed. O gazelle of the valley ravine at the watering-place, grazing the colocynth in summer and the sweet grass. You read at its lower bank fresh green hillocks, and you rest at its upper part in a hollow thicket. You have made anguish follow my heart, just as I once followed hopes in you that were mere adornment. How many a gloating rival will rejoice if you do not disappoint the hope of one who hoped you would disappoint. Do not forget nine months that exhausted me with diligence, and exhausted me on the road to you and more. With odes that your sea could not satisfy their watering — had they watered at the rock, they would have split it open! God, what intercession was strongest at first, yet how weak in the end! I fear that a moment of your gaze at my outcome will have you called the procrastinator, and I named the importunate. The least of my ambition was once thought too small — spring's abundance came, and now I settle for summer. Your winds blew upon me as a gentle southerly breeze, but when I budded, they returned as a cold north wind. If you do not grant favor and do not see that I am worthy of it, I at least ask that you be fair. What excuse has one whose generosity is obedient to him, yet whose nature makes him see it as forced effort? You have been excessive in denying me, while your very habit has prevented your reins from giving freely in excess. God protects you from turning away or letting what has long been hoped of you weaken and fail. Do not turn your generosity away from one who has left no room in his praise of you to praise another. Train a youth in generosity and you will find odes whose wild verses have found in you a straightener of shafts. Do not accept mediocrity, lest you anger wild verses that stirred you only so they might pierce you like a sharpened blade. Dispel suspicion with certainty — for certainty does not wear out what doubled praise has preserved. How many a noble, gracious man's generosity was grasped at, then delayed, until the face of his gift turned its back! I have left no means untried with you — striving and persisting, dazzling and charming and displaying wit. Yet I see you deflecting my rightful claim — perhaps I have become a burden; unblamed, I ask you then to lighten it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a wide cloak or veil that envelops one fully
lowered, covering, thick and spread out
to peel away or strip off (said of darkness giving way to whiteness)
unripe fruit of the arak tree, known as a grazing shrub
ornament, embellishment; here figuratively: illusion, false hope
wild verses or images that are strikingly original and difficult to tame
one who is importunate, who presses and pesters with demands
a cold, violent north wind

context: A complex panegyric-complaint poem in which Abu Tammam addresses a patron who has grown white-haired — opening with a meditation on aging and desire, then pivoting to reproach the patron for withholding promised reward after nine months of composition. The poem moves between ghazal sentiment and sharp madih-complaint, with Abu Tammam urging his patron to restore his generosity before the poet's praise turns to reproach.

titleEn: The Eye of the Yearning Youth Spoke

verses.sadr: ذو اعتداء على ثراء فتى الجو

translationEn: The eye of the yearning young man spoke, complaining through an overflow of streaming tears. Tears translated onto the pages of his cheeks lines of letters composed in order. Even though the homelands have grown distant and time has consumed both the familiar and the beloved, and cheerfulness has been exchanged for sorrow after joy in spring and summer — my consolation is that my honor is preserved, my path to water is easy, and generosity is my ally. And my knowledge, despite my youth, of the vicissitudes and turns of time — riding the affairs of this life in the arena of days, toward rescue or toward death. I rely upon the wealth of a young man of generosity, noble in deeds, and son of the noble. Would that I knew what troubles you about me — you who surpass the discernment of a philosopher! Seize this opportunity that will gladden you — through cultivating good deeds and kindness. I am one of noble speech, offering speech in exchange for giving, and noble in restraint when one withholds. I do not care, when your affairs distress you, how the hands of fate carve against me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who is distressed, yearning, overcome with longing
flowing, gushing; said of tears that pour abundantly
to translate or express; here: tears translating emotion into visible lines
the vicissitudes and turns of fate or time
the arena or racetrack; figuratively the field of striving
deaths, mortal dangers, fatal ends
cultivating, performing, fashioning (good deeds)

context: A self-presentational poem in which Abu Tammam opens with elegiac sentiment about separation and lost joys, then pivots to assert his own worth and nobility to a patron. The poem builds toward a request that the patron recognize the poet's talent and seize the opportunity to be immortalized in verse.

titleEn: A Brother Whose Company Turned Bitter

verses.sadr: لو مت لم تعدل وفاتك بغتة

translationEn: A brother whose manner and taste I found repugnant, and whose harsh leading and driving I grew weary of. I gave him, after closeness, a severance that tightened the knot of his belt over his sighs. Go then — for how many a companion I have parted with before you, in whose whites of the eyes I saw the very form of injustice. Had you died, your sudden death would not have equaled a dream that frightens me with the day of our parting. A man's closest friends are those whose eyes search diligently on his behalf for his sincerity and his hypocrisy. Let every man judge by his servants — for they are his character traits reflected in their own characters.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to find repugnant, to be repelled by something ugly or disagreeable
a belt or girdle; tightening it over sighs suggests suffocation of grief
the white of the eye; seeing injustice there means it is plainly visible
searching carefully, digging deep to investigate
hypocrisy, two-facedness, dissimulation
young servants or attendants; their conduct reflects their master's character

context: A short poem of estrangement (hija) in which Abu Tammam denounces a former friend whose true character finally revealed itself. The poem ends with a moral aphorism: a man's servants mirror his own character, suggesting the companion's household was as corrupt as he was.

titleAr: أجميل ما لك لا تجيب أخاكا
titleEn: O Jamil, Why Do You Not Answer?

verses.sadr: أجميل ما لك لا تجيب أخاكا
أغنى ظفرت به فإني في غنى
بل لا نسيت - ولا ألومك - خلتي

translationEn: O Jamil, why will you not answer your brother? What in God's name has befallen you! Have you attained some wealth? For I am in no need of the blessing God has given you. Rather, have you forgotten our friendship — and I do not blame you — but if you have, a new event will make you forget even yourself. You will one day reproach your poor judgment; it is a misguided opinion that has long brought you to ruin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: what has happened to you, what calamity or circumstance has struck you
wealth, enrichment; here used ironically — 'did you gain riches?'
my friendship, my intimate bond with you
misguided, errant, one who has strayed from the right path
to ruin someone, cause their downfall or destruction

context: A brief rebuke addressed to a man named Jamil, likely a former associate who has become distant or failed to respond to the poet. Abu Tammam combines injured pride with a cutting prediction: Jamil's poor judgment will eventually destroy him.

titleEn: I Witness: You Wore Abu Said's Generosity

verses.ajuz: إذا ما غب يوما صار مالا

translationEn: I testify that you, O Abu Said, have clothed yourself in noble qualities that dazzle even the loftiest of honors. When time's heat blazes, the hands of his generosity blaze and shade the whole world. When a man's soul seemed mean to us, we saw in the arena of his bounty a majesty and generosity that towered. Men protected you from adversity — men who never extended a right hand to action, nor a left. Was it not when you raised me in your estimation, and my condition around me changed in the shelter of your vast shade? And tribes near and distant gathered around me as my dependents, though I had been their dependent. For I have become the most generous of them in giving, while before you I was the most asking of them. When they intercede with me, I spare them neither the humiliation of the cheek nor the sandal. I speak haltingly about their needs whether they are light or heavy when I bring them to you. When need sets its hands loose, I have made your refusal the tether that restrains it. Where then are my odes of you — those that refuse to allow me to be humiliated or brought low? They are a lawful magic for whoever gathers them; I have never seen a magic that was lawful before this. Do not let your pool grow turbid for me, for I stretch toward you hopes that are long and far-reaching. Preserve my standing with you — for prestige, when it lingers a while, becomes wealth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to dazzle, overwhelm, overpower with brightness or magnificence
shelter, shade; protection under a patron's care
dependents, those who rely on another for sustenance
to stammer or stumble when speaking; to be hesitant with words
a pool or pond; here figuratively: the patron's pool of generosity
lawful magic; a phrase for eloquent poetry that enchants without harm
prestige, rank, social standing

context: A panegyric to Abu Said in which Abu Tammam testifies to his patron's transformative generosity: through the patron's support, the poet himself became a giver rather than a beggar. The poem uses the memorable phrase 'lawful magic' for his own poetry, arguing that his odes are a precious asset that protect both the poet's honor and the patron's fame.

titleAr: قد عرفناه دلائل المنع أو ما
titleEn: We Have Known the Signs of Withholding

verses.sadr: قد عرفناه دلائل المنع أو ما
فاجأتنا كدراء لم تسب من تسم
من عقار لا ريحها نفحة المس
لا تهدى سبل العروق ولا تنسل
وهي نزر لو أنها من دموع الصبم

verses.ajuz: بعد كد من ماء وجه البخيل !
ت بها رحمة على ابن السبيل ؟ !

verses.sadr: قد كتبنا لك الأمان فما تس

translationEn: We have recognized the signs of withholding — or something resembling withholding — in the delay of the messenger. We were exposed to shame before the raisins (the wine host) by what became apparent of the ugliness of the wine's face. It surprised us as a turbid wine, not steeped from pure coloring nor from sweet water. From a vintage whose scent carries no breath of musk and whose cheek is not smooth and flowing. It does not guide the pathways of the veins, nor does it flow into a joint without a guide. It is meager — were it tears of the patient one, it would not quench the burning of thirst. As though the fingers had wrung it out with great effort from the water of the miser's face! Was it given for the sake of spiritual merit, or as charity out of pity for the wayfarer? We have written you a guarantee of safety — do not ask it for the duration of this long age. How many a concealed generous man we have tested, judging his abundance by his small portions!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wine; specifically a wine that engulfs the senses with its aroma
turbid, murky; describing poor-quality, cloudy wine
the natural red color or dye of wine; its vivid hue
sweet, easy-flowing water; used in the Quran for a spring in paradise
the burning heat of thirst or intense longing
the miser; wringing wine from his face is a satirical image of extreme stinginess
concealed, hidden; here a generous man whose generosity is not outwardly apparent

context: A satirical poem (hija) in which Abu Tammam mocks poor hospitality — specifically bad wine served by a stingy host. The poem shifts from vivid wine description into a broader satire on miserliness, ending with a sardonic observation about testing generosity through small samples.

titleEn: Strange That Your Face Turns Away

verses.sadr: أو لا ترى أن الطلاقة جنة
verses.ajuz: من سوء ما تجني الظنون ومعقل

verses.sadr: إن تعط وجها كاسفا من تحته

translationEn: By your life, it is strange that your face turns away from me while you come toward me in the face of your actions! You began with piety, and the door of your house is open to all creation — yet your face is locked. Do you not see that a cheerful countenance is a shield and fortress against the evil that suspicion reaps? The ornament of a generous deed is that its doer should have a speech full of resonance and a lively, animated eye. And an affection folded yet spread out — its fulfillment is constantly sought and worked toward. If you give with a gloomy face, beneath which lie generosity and forbearance — that is a character that should not be ignored. For many a night cloud heavy with rain has poured abundantly while its face does not flash with lightning!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: turning away, averted; said of one who avoids eye contact or shuns another
cheerfulness of face, brightness of expression, pleasant countenance
resonant, ringing; said of voice or speech that has presence and impact
moving, animated, lively (said of the eye)
gloomy, dark, eclipsed; a face clouded with displeasure
a night cloud that brings rain; rain that comes in darkness
the side of a cloud; here the cloud's face that pours rain without lightning

context: A short poem of gentle reproach in which Abu Tammam addresses a generous patron who deeds are praiseworthy but whose demeanor is cold and averted. The final image — a night cloud that rains without flashing — elegantly captures the paradox of hidden generosity expressed without a warm face.

titleEn: I Am Ashamed That My Certainty Should Show Doubt

verses.sadr: وما زال لي علم إذا ما نصصته
وإن يك عدا عن سواك إليك بي
verses.ajuz: رحيل فلي في الأرض عنك رحيل

verses.sadr: أبى الحزم لي مكثا بدار مضيعة
وإن امرءا ضنت يداه على امرىء

translationEn: I am ashamed that my certainty should be seen to have a path of doubt about anything. And I have always had a knowledge — when I examine it carefully — that refinement in you is scarce. If it was a journey away from others toward you that brought me here, then I also have in this earth a journey away from you. Prudence refuses to let me remain in a house of waste, with a she-camel whose sires are Shadqam and Jadil. After what has passed — after which no blame should remain — should you say of a man of honor that he is foolish? I will cut the tethers of reproach with a speech that is short in form yet long in the effort of thought behind it. And truly, a man whose hands are stingy toward another man in granting what another's hand would grant — is a miser.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my certainty, my firm conviction
to examine carefully, to push to the utmost, to scrutinize thoroughly
resoluteness, prudence, firm decision-making
a famous Arab stallion; reference to noble camel lineage
the tethers of reproach; cutting them means ending the complaint
his hands were stingy; to be miserly, to withhold

context: A poem of departure and reproach in which Abu Tammam tells a patron he is leaving, framing his departure as a rational decision rather than wounded pride. The poem ends with a proverbial statement about miserliness — a man who withholds from another what others would freely give is simply a miser.

titleEn: Know This, O Unteachable Man
translationEn: Know — and you are a man not easily taught — and understand — may I be your ransom — though you are not easily made to understand. That cultivating a good deed, if not carried out completely, is like a cloak not yet hemmed. And gratitude, if not accompanied by a visible deed of kindness, is like writing that you can read but that lacks diacritical marks. You have elaborated greatly in speech — I have now saddled you in the nobility of deeds, so bridle yourself!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: carried out completely, brought to full perfection
a striped cloak or garment; hemming it (إعلام) completes it
a text with diacritical dots; without them Arabic letters are unreadable
to elaborate in multiple styles, to use various artistic techniques
to saddle a mount; here: to set the patron up for action
put on the bridle; stop talking and act — a command to follow through

context: A short and sharp poem of advice: Abu Tammam tells a patron that incomplete generosity, like an unfinished garment or undotted writing, is meaningless. The final couplet is one of his most memorable: having saddled you in generosity, now bridle — stop talking and act.

titleAr: لا يحمد السجل حتى يحكم الوذم
titleEn: The Bucket Is Not Praised Until the Rope Is Sound

verses.sadr: لا يحمد السجل حتى يحكم الوذم
كنا وكنت على عهد مضى سلفا
حتى إذا لم نخف نقض الهوى وصفت
كوارد الخمس شهر القيظ جاد له
أحين قمت من الأيام في كبد

translationEn: The bucket is not praised until its rope is sound, and there is no good in one without the blessings that connect. In gems there are similar stones that resemble each other — but light and darkness do not mix. Many a calamity has struck two intimates and split them apart from their friendship, while the ties still knit together. A covenant shapes their two hearts, renewed by the length of time, and age does not corrode it. They condemned the ingratitude and returned the fullness of their forbearance, and they resumed closeness — generosity made an exception of them. We were once, and you were once, forebears on a covenant that has passed — and at the end of estrangement comes regret. We have two kinships in two hearts that love — both manifest and hidden — brought them back to purity. Until when we no longer feared the unraveling of affection, friendship became clear to us, its very water pure and flowing. And we were in the shelter of a state of mutual support, each committed to the passion of the lovers. Like camels arriving at the watering place on the fifth day in the heat of summer, for which the spring was generous and the acacia tree extended its shade. Exposure of a need whose sanctity a patronage squandered, while the soul's impulses are suspect! Was it when you rose among the days prominent in the belly of events, like the beacon lit by the fire of a bonfire? You plunged yourself into a thick, pitch-black darkness, and blessings corrupted you in the eyes of your brothers! It is a worldly life — but a worldly life that shall pass away, and the end of all living is death and decay!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a large leather bucket used to draw water from a well
the leather thongs or ties that attach a bucket to its rope
watering on the fifth day; camels that go five days without water then drink
a subterranean spring found by digging in sandy ground
pitch dark, deeply darkened; total darkness
a beacon, landmark, or signal fire used as a guide
to come to an end, be severed, pass away

context: A meditation on friendship, estrangement, and reconciliation. Abu Tammam opens with proverbs about the need for sound foundations, then narrates the reunion of two estranged companions — only to pivot to sharp reproach: the addressee's prosperity has made him forget his brothers. The poem ends with a reminder of mortality.

titleEn: Muhammad ibn Said, Lend Me Your Ear

verses.sadr: لم تسق بعد الهوى ماء على ظمأ
من كل بيت يكاد الميت يفهمه

verses.ajuz: إلا زهير وقد أصغى له هرم

verses.sadr: ما زال يخضع مذ أورقت لي عدة
verses.ajuz: فكيف يصنع لو قد أثمرت نعم
وقد حكى سوء ظن أن ذا حلم !
ليس العلا طللا يزري به القدم !

translationEn: O Muhammad ibn Said, lend me your ear — for your ear is not deaf to noble qualities. You have not been given, after passion, water for your thirst like the water of a clear spring that understanding pours for you — from every verse such that a dead man would almost grasp its beauty, and the page and pen envy it. No comparison between me and you exists when it is recited, save Zuhayr when Haram listened to him. With every verse that traverses the mind and dominates it, so that the listener seems bewitched or touched by madness. The hands of the family of Sahl, whenever I sought their gift, did in times of drought what continuous rains do not do. A people you see jealously guarding their glory, so that high deeds seem to them sacred ground. Time has turned against me bearing its gloom, its chest of grief boiling and blazing. It has not ceased to submit since my resources budded — how much more would it submit if they bore fruit! So let action awaken and settle the sleep of words — for suspicion of good sense has said this man is a dreamer! And do not say that age has diminished his need — nobility is not a ruined abode that age erodes!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: lend me (your ear); to listen attentively
a source of pure, clear water; a clear, unmuddied spring
zealously jealous, fiercely protective; guarding glory like a prized possession
continuous gentle rains; the slow, persistent rains of sustained blessing
to boil, to seethe; the chest of time boiling with hidden resentment
ruined dwelling place; the trope of the abandoned campsite in Arabic poetry
Zuhayr ibn Abi Sulma, the great pre-Islamic poet, used here as the standard of poetic excellence

context: A panegyric to Muhammad ibn Said of the Sahl family, opening with praise for Abu Tammam's own poetry — comparing himself to Zuhayr reciting before Haram — before praising the patron's family and demanding recognition. The final line is a memorable defense against those who dismiss long-serving poets as past their prime.

titleEn: My Tribe and Ubaydallah's Are Reunited

verses.ajuz: هل كان عمرو على الصمصام يتهم

verses.sadr: سيفي الذي حده من جانبي أبدا
verses.ajuz: ناب ومن جانب القوم العدى خذم

verses.sadr: سعاية من رجال لا طباخ بهم
verses.ajuz: قالوا بما جهلوا فينا وما علموا
أخلاقنا الغر فينا غير ما زعموا

verses.sadr: إنا خدمنا القلى جهلا بنا وعمى

translationEn: My tribe and the tribe of Ubaydallah are reunited — how could the leg and the foot differ from each other? They accused me of failing to preserve my sword Samsama — was Amr ibn Madikarib ever accused with regard to Samsama? My sword whose edge from my side is ever biting, and from the side of the people's enemies is ever sharp. We tasted estrangement, and when our reins were led together again, kinship between us moaned like a yearning camel. Estrangement will come to know that we take our revenge for its harm and injustice with sweet reunion. The faces were scowling — but the hearts were smiling all along. There were informers among men of no worth who spoke of us with ignorance of what they did not know. They schemed — but when our estrangement met and passed, our radiant characters proved to be other than what they claimed. And souls that had been one breathed deeply again, for a single father whose nose was held high in pride. We had served enmity in ignorance and blindness — but today we all together serve reconciliation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: healed, reunited, knit back together; said of a wound or rift that has closed
a famous sword belonging to the hero Amr ibn Madikarib; a byword for a legendary blade
sharp, cutting keenly; said of a blade's edge on the side facing enemies
our reins or halters; leading them together means guiding the two parties back to each other
a she-camel whose calf has been taken; she moans with longing — an image of yearning
malicious informing, scheming denunciation, tale-bearing to cause harm
loftiness of the nose; pride, nobility of lineage and bearing

context: A poem of reconciliation with Ubaydallah, celebrating the reunion of two estranged kinsmen or allies. Abu Tammam blames malicious informers for the estrangement, insists the two parties' hearts were always aligned even when their faces showed coldness, and ends with a declaration that they now jointly serve reconciliation.

titleAr: أبا القاسم أسلم في وفود من القسم
titleEn: Abu al-Qasim, May You Live Among Oaths of Safety

verses.sadr: أبا القاسم أسلم في وفود من القسم
verses.ajuz: ولا زال من حاربته دامي الكلم

verses.sadr: رأيتك ترعى الجود من كل وجهة
verses.ajuz: وتبني بناء المجد في خطة النجم
على الضخم آراء لدى الحادث الضخم
إذا جف أطراف البخيل من الأزم
وأكرم في اللأواء عودا من الكرم

verses.sadr: ففيم تركت النصف في الود بعدما
verses.ajuz: رآه الورى خيرا من النصف في الحكم ؟

verses.sadr: أإياي جارى القوم في الشعر ضلة
verses.ajuz: وقد عاينوا تلك القلائد من نظمي !

verses.sadr: طلعت طلوع الشمس في كل تلعة
وما أنا بالغيران من دون جاره
verses.ajuz: إذ أنا لم اصبح غيورا على العلم

verses.sadr: أبى ذاك صبر لا يقيل على الأذى
verses.ajuz: فواقا ونفس لا تمرغ في الظلم

verses.sadr: وإني إذا ما الحلم أحوج لاحيا
verses.ajuz: إلى سفه أفضلت فضلا على حلمي

verses.sadr: تظن ظنون السوء بي إن لقيتني
verses.ajuz: ولا وتري فيما كرهت ولا سهمي
فإنك تمحوها بما فيك من شكم

verses.sadr: وما خير حلم لم تشبه شراسة
verses.ajuz: وما خير لحم لا يكون على عظم
فمن خلق طلق ومن خلق جهم !

verses.sadr: فإن لم تطيبا لي جميعا فإنه
verses.ajuz: نهى عمر عن أكل أدمين في أدم !

translationEn: O Abu al-Qasim, may you live safely, visited by delegations bearing oaths of loyalty — and may whoever wars against you always be wound-bleeding. I have seen you tending to generosity from every direction, building the edifice of glory in the constellation of stars. A man of character — easy, handsome, commanding — fashioned from both strength and yielding tenderness. When a calamity strikes, you turn its vicissitudes into weighty counsel at the moment of great events. Your two hands are for us both months of spring together, when the edges of the miser's fingertips dry from clenching. Sweeter in friendship than shade and sunlight, and more noble in hardship than the vine in yielding its best. Then why did you abandon equity in affection, after all people saw it as better than equity in legal judgment? Do people really compete with me in poetry out of error, having seen those necklaces of my verse! I have risen like the sun rising over every highland, and shone forth like the star Simak high above the foe. I am not jealous of my neighbor's advantage — for I have never been one to be jealous of knowledge itself. It has been close to my heart for thirty years — the polisher of my mind and the one who relieves my sorrows. That bond refuses to let patience rest even a moment from bearing harm — and my soul refuses to wallow in injustice. When forbearance compels a man of reproach to stoop to folly, I have risen higher than my own forbearance. You think ill of me when you meet me, yet I have no arrow in your dislike, no shaft. You wince at my jesting, yet you accept a poem whose very words are shaped like insults. If you are sometimes hard of bit, you erase that with your own reticence. What good is forbearance unstained by a little fierceness, and what good is meat that has no bone? Are they anything but noble characters that balanced themselves — one character easy and open, another stern and dark? They are stars: this one for illumination when it rises, darkness dispels before it — and that one for hurling stones at the enemy. If neither pleases you fully together, remember that Umar forbade eating two relishes with one bread!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: oath, sworn pledge; votive delegations bearing oaths of safety and loyalty
the bit of a bridle; figuratively: harshness, firmness of character
reticence, restraint, the quality of holding one's tongue wisely
the star Spica or Arcturus; one of the brightest stars, used for navigation
a polisher of swords; here figuratively: what sharpens and polishes the mind
two relishes in one bread; a reference to Umar's prohibition against combining two condiments
hardship, adversity, difficult times

context: A long panegyric and complaint poem to Abu al-Qasim, opening with lavish praise of his patron's character and generosity, then shifting to defend Abu Tammam's own poetry against rivals, and finally to a candid dialogue about the tensions in their relationship. The poem's ending — citing Umar's prohibition of two condiments — wittily argues that both the poet's earnestness and humor cannot coexist with the patron's rejection.

titleEn: Were It Not for Old Bonds and Sanctity
translationEn: Were it not for our old bond and a sanctity that has been honored, I would have severed all ties between me and Hisham. He does not honor the sanctity of old literary companionship — and I see that he is ignorant even of the sanctity of Islam. It is as if our affection for him, and the devoted service we rendered him, were nothing but a dream among dreams. The shifting behavior of brothers, once you have uncovered their true face, will make you forget the long shifting of days!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the old bond, the long-standing relationship or covenant
a sanctity that is honored and respected, inviolable regard
the sanctity of the Islamic bond; the religious duty of respect between Muslims
our devoted service, our handmaidens; here figuratively: the service and dedication given to him
the shifting conduct of brothers; their changing faces once tested

context: A short poem of estrangement from Hisham, in which Abu Tammam reproaches a man who has violated both the bonds of literary brotherhood and the broader sanctity of Muslim fellowship. The final line is a powerful aphorism: the betrayal of a friend erases the sting of fate's own inconstancy.

titleEn: Your Messenger on the Day of Battle Is the Lance
translationEn: Your messenger on the day of battle is the Khattiyya lance, with the bright cutting sword riding behind it. Whoever sleeps deliberately from a noble deed — you are not one who sleeps from it throughout all time. None has been seen in his clan equal to him — more just to the wronged against the wrongdoer. But he delays a right that has already been ratified for me by a judge!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a lance from Khatt, a port in Arabia known for fine spears
the bright cutting sword; a white, keen-edged blade
battle, the tumult of war, the clamor of combat
the clan, close kindred; the immediate tribal family
official registration or ratification by a judge; a legal document of entitlement

context: A short panegyric with a concluding sting: Abu Tammam praises the addressee's martial valor and justice, only to end by noting that the man delays a legally ratified right owed to the poet — transforming praise into pointed demand.

titleAr: ما ابيض وجه المرء في طلب العلى
titleEn: No Man's Face Whitens in Seeking Glory

verses.sadr: ما ابيض وجه المرء في طلب العلى

translationEn: No man's face becomes white with honor in seeking glory until his face has blackened with the dust of the wastelands. And it is true that provision seeks out its people — but only through the striving of one who is wearied and exhausted!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: high honor, loftiness, glory
the desert wastelands, empty expanses traveled in hardship
provision, sustenance, livelihood ordained by God
exhausted, worn out, one who has toiled to the point of fatigue
a stratagem, means, device; here: earnest effort and ingenuity

context: A two-verse aphoristic poem (hikma) in which Abu Tammam refutes the fatalistic notion that provision comes without effort — affirming that even if destiny is written, it still requires a man to exhaust himself in pursuing it.

titleEn: A Blamer Whom I Blamed for His Blaming

verses.ajuz: رأي ابن دهر غرقا في خبله
قد دان ذو الفضل له بفضله
ذا عنق في المجد لم يحله
والشعر ما لم يك عند أهله !

translationEn: A blamer I blamed for his blaming — and he thought that I was ignorant from his own ignorance. No defrauded man is defrauded like one robbed of his mind — who will ever give you a day your brother whole and true? I have worn the cloak of a simple man — leave me to the fool's opinion of a worldly man drowning in his own confusion. I know better than he his camels' driving songs — the hands of separation have played with his cloak. One who enjoys fully what he carries, able to bear it, drawn out straight like a sword from its scabbard. His ambition was born before him — the man of excellence has sworn by his own excellence to him. Like colocynth: whoever tastes it cannot find it bearable except by sheltering in his shade. A benefactor of great wealth, giving its substance — what he gathers from his lawful and his licit alike. He makes granting the least of his paths, and makes a distant land his home. I aimed at him in the night journey with my noble verses — a mature powerful camel at the peak of strength. One like me traveled in one like the night journey with one like his worth — a king in his grandeur and nobility, yet a commoner in his speech and conduct. I offered my praise hoping to receive its equal in return. But he cut the rope of my hope from its root, after having enslaved me with his procrastination. Then he came apologizing through his ignorance — one with a neck in glory no one has bejeweled. He wonders at my wonder and his own miserliness, watching me in his seriousness and his jesting — with the glance of a prisoner looking at the links of his own shackles — as if I had come to him with his dismissal. O you who alone stand singular in justice, whom I clothed in wealth — do not grow weary of it! How wasted is a scabbard without its blade — and how wasted is poetry if not in the hands of those who deserve it!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a blamer, one who reproaches and counsels against love or rashness
a simple, naïve person; one who appears innocent or artless
the colocynth tree; its fruit is intensely bitter — an image of one unbearable without his protection
mature, fully-grown camels at the peak of strength and reliability
night travel; journeying in the darkness to reach a destination
the scabbard or sheath of a sword; a fine sheath is wasted without the blade
the links of a shackle; the prisoner staring at his own chains — image of helpless fixation

context: A long poem of complaint and self-assertion in the rajaz meter, in which Abu Tammam describes his experience with a patron who admired him, delayed his reward, and then came with hollow excuses. The poem moves from dismissing a blamer to praising the patron's qualities, then pivoting to sharp complaint, ending with the famous image: poetry without a worthy patron is a sword in an empty scabbard.

titleEn: Is There Among You a Young Man to Tell Me of Myself?

verses.ajuz: بما شربت مشروبة الراح من ذهني
غدت وهي أولى من فؤادي بعزمتي

verses.sadr: ورحت بما في الدن أولى من الدن
إذا اشتعلت في الطاس والكاس نارها
verses.ajuz: صليت بها من راحتي ناعم لدن
ذكرت بها أيام يوسف في الحسن
سلافا كماء الجفن وهي من الجفن

verses.sadr: تقلب روح المرء في كل وجهة
verses.ajuz: وتدخل منه حيث شاءت بلا إذن
لنا كل نوع من قرى العين والأذن

verses.sadr: لنا وتر منه إذا ما استحثه
verses.ajuz: فصيح ولحن في أمان من اللحن

verses.sadr: ظللنا بها في جنة غاب نحسها
نعمنا بها في بيت أروع ماجد
فتى شق من عود المحامد عوده
verses.ajuz: كما اشتق له اسما من الحسن

translationEn: Is there among you a living youth who can tell me of myself — what the drink of wine has consumed of my mind? In the dark gatherings of night when they call out to each other — it is a resolve like the sword, except that it has been made into pruning rods for the causes of time. It has become worthier of my heart than my own resolve. And I returned, what the wine jar held more worthy of me than the jar — its cup has truly left me and my own reality behind. An impossibility — by the truth of my deeds which resemble suspicion — it has deceived me, and the clouds, and I was not the first to offer heedlessness to the darkness, whose like can shatter the pillars of mountains. When its fire blazed in the bowl and cup, I warmed myself at it from my palm — a tender, supple sapling. A companion of youth whose cheeks hold beauty — in that beauty I was reminded of the days of Joseph in his comeliness. When we gestured to him, he circled it — a vintage like the water of an eye, and itself from the eye. It turns a man's spirit in every direction and enters him wherever it wishes without permission. And our musician — delicate of fingertip — has with him every variety of hospitality for eye and ear. We have a string from him that, when plucked, is eloquent and melodious, in safety from dissonance. And in a meadow lush with growth, its streams and blossoms dyed the color of oil — we found shade in it, in a grove whose density we sensed around us, its gardens reminding us of the Garden of Eden. We enjoyed all this in the house of a brave, noble man of the people — one who spurns baseness and feebleness. A youth whose own lineage was cleft from the wood of praises, just as his name was derived from beauty.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wine; pure wine, the intoxicating drink
the wine jar or large vessel in which wine is stored
the first pressing of grapes; the purest, finest wine
the eyelid; also a vine — the wine is like tears and from the vine
delicate of fingertip; said of a skilled musician whose fingers are soft and sensitive
rich in vegetation, abundantly planted; a lush garden meadow
feebleness of mind, mental deficiency, weakness of character

context: A wine and gathering poem (khamriyya/ghazal) set at a convivial night assembly, describing wine, a beautiful wine-server, a musician, and a lush garden — all in the house of a praised host. Note: the source text shows OCR disruption in several verses due to page breaks, and the verse sequence may be partially reconstructed.

titleEn: Did the Nobles of Maad and Madhij Ever Gather?

verses.sadr: بل اليمن استعلت لدى كل موطن

translationEn: Did the nobles of Maad and Madhij ever gather at a battle except with one of us as their commander? Rather, Yemen has risen supreme at every station, and Tayyi has become its crown and throne. The rumps of my horses in battle are sacred — their breasts and throats alone bear the wounds. It is forbidden for our lances to strike a fleeing back — our lances' own chests shatter in valor against the chests of the foe.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a great Arab tribal confederation, ancestors of the northern Arabs
a major Yemeni tribal group
the tribe of Tayyi, a noble Arab tribe of northern Arabia
the rumps or hindquarters of horses; attacking from behind is dishonorable
the breast or chest area; the chest of the horse bears the wounds, not the back
to shatter, to break; the lances' chests shatter against enemy chests in frontal assault

context: A short poem of tribal boasting (fakhr) in which Abu Tammam praises the tribe of Tayyi and Yemen for their supremacy in battle. The central image — that horses' backs are untouched while their chests bear all wounds — encapsulates the tribal code of honor: only cowards attack from behind.

titleEn: If Others Changed Under Wealth's Influence

verses.ajuz: إني امرؤ ليس ترضى الضيم لي الهمم

translationEn: If captivity and wealth have changed others, they have not changed me from my original nature and resolve. When time couches its full weight upon me, I host it with patience and resolve — my generosity is the hosting. If the injustices of the ages press down upon me, I train my soul to endure until the injustice lifts. Through all of this that circumstances have granted me — I am a man whose ambitions do not accept humiliation for me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: captivity, being taken prisoner; here referring to changed conditions
original nature, innate character, the root of one's being
the chest or full weight of a beast; time couching its full chest upon one
hospitality offered to a guest; here: hosting time's burden with patience
humiliation, injustice suffered passively; that which crushes dignity
high ambitions, noble aspirations; what refuses to accept dishonor

context: A short poem of self-assertion in which Abu Tammam declares his unchanging character despite the vicissitudes of fate — neither captivity nor wealth has altered his nature. The poem is notable for its image of hosting time's weight like a guest, and for its closing declaration that his high ambitions refuse humiliation.

titleAr: إذا ما شبت حسن الدين منك بصالح الأدب
titleEn: When Religion Is Perfected by Noble Conduct

verses.sadr: إذا ما شبت حسن الدين منك بصالح الأدب
verses.ajuz: فممن شئت كن فلقد فلحت بأكرم النسب

translationEn: When you blend beauty of religion with righteous conduct and noble manners — then be of whatever lineage you wish, for you have succeeded with the most noble of ancestries. Reform your own self — that is all — and leave me out of the old claims of a father.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to mix, blend, or combine (one quality with another)
refinement, noble conduct, literary culture and good manners
to succeed, to attain salvation and true prosperity
lineage, genealogy, noble descent
the old claims of a father; ancestral boasts and inherited pride

context: A brief aphoristic poem in which Abu Tammam argues that true nobility comes from combining religion and conduct, not from lineage. The poem is characteristic of his moralizing tendency: earned virtue matters more than inherited rank.

titleEn: We Seek What Has Departed — Farewell
translationEn: We pursue something that has departed and bid it farewell — how far it is from ever returning or coming back! I was roughened by discipline in understanding and in speech, and softened by the days in flexibility and in the base of the neck. And the days came searching for a place to lay you low — so turn back, now that you have been certain of a resting place.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: how far, how impossible; an expression of utter remoteness or impossibility
discipline, training, the cultivation of refinement and conduct
the side of the neck; softened there — meaning made pliant, tamed by time
the jugular vein or side-vein of the neck; also: what is most subtle and hidden
a place of falling or being struck down; the place of one's overthrow
a resting place, a place to lie down; here: the grave or place of final rest

context: A short valedictory poem of three verses in which Abu Tammam reflects on the passage of time, the hardening and softening that experience brings, and the approach of death. The final verse — the days searching for a place to lay one low — makes this one of his meditations on mortality.
